title: Python Basics Checkpoint
description: Comprehensive assessment of Python fundamentals covering variables, data types, operations, and basic control flow.

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid variable name in Python?

options.explanation: This follows Python naming conventions: lowercase with underscores
Variable names cannot start with a number
Variable names cannot contain hyphens; use underscores instead
Variable names cannot contain spaces

questions.question:
What will be printed by this code?
x = 5
x = 10
x = x + 3
print(x)

options.explanation: x starts at 5, becomes 10, then x + 3 = 13

questions.question: Create a variable named 'motor_speed' with value 75, then create another variable 'final_speed' that increases motor_speed by 15. Print final_speed.
questions.explanation: This tests understanding of variable creation, reassignment, and arithmetic operations.

questions.question: What is the difference between 5 and 5.0 in Python?

options.text: 5 is an integer (int), 5.0 is a float
options.explanation: 5 is stored as int, 5.0 is stored as float; they have equal value but different types

options.text: 5.0 is an integer, 5 is a float

questions.question: What is the type of the result when you calculate 10 / 2 in Python 3?

options.explanation: In Python 3, / always returns a float, even for whole number division
The / operator performs floating-point division and returns a float
In Python 3, / always returns float. Use // for integer division

questions.question: Which of these is a string?

options.explanation: This is an integer
The quotes make it a string of the characters 4 and 2
This is a list
This is a float
This would only be correct if operations were evaluated left-to-right without precedence
Multiplication has higher precedence: 5 * 2 = 10, then 10 + 10 = 20
String concatenation combines strings: 'Hello' + ' ' + 'Robot' = 'Hello Robot'
The space character ' ' is included in the concatenation
String concatenation is valid in Python

questions.question: Create variables for robot battery status. Create 'battery_percent' with 85, 'voltage' with 7.4, then print an f-string: 'Battery: {battery_percent}% ({voltage}V)'
questions.explanation: This assesses ability to work with mixed data types and f-string formatting.

options.explanation: 10 is greater than 5, so the condition is true and 'True' is printed
50 is not > 75, but it IS > 25, so 'Medium' is printed
Only one branch of the if/elif/else executes

questions.question:
Write code that:
1. Creates a variable 'battery_level' with value 45
2. Uses an if/elif/else statement to print:
   - 'Battery critical' if battery < 20
   - 'Battery low' if battery < 50
   - 'Battery good' otherwise
questions.explanation: This tests understanding of conditional logic with multiple branches.

breakdown.description: Understanding int, float, and string types

excellent.message: Excellent work! You have a strong grasp of Python fundamentals. You're ready to move on to more advanced topics.

good.message: Good job! You understand the core concepts. Review the areas where you had difficulty and practice before moving forward.

needsWork.message: You need more practice with Python basics. Review the lessons and try the exercises again. Don't hesitate to ask for help!
